annotations.label: Physics

anchor.snippet: Physics is the scientific study of matter

annotations.note: Physics as a scientific discipline is not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Physicist

anchor.snippet: A scientist who specializes in the field of physics is called a physicist

annotations.note: A profession is not a mathematical concept formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Etymology of physics

anchor.snippet: The word physics comes from the Latin physica

annotations.note: Etymology is a linguistic fact with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Astronomy

anchor.snippet: Astronomy is one of the oldest natural sciences

annotations.note: Astronomy as a science is not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Atomism confirmed

anchor.snippet: atomism was found to be correct approximately 2000 years after it was proposed by Leucippus

annotations.note: A historical physical claim, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Theory of four elements

anchor.snippet: He explained ideas such as motion (and gravity ) with the theory of four elements

annotations.note: An obsolete physical theory with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Natural place of elements

anchor.snippet: Aristotle believed that each of the four classical elements (air, fire, water, earth) had its own natural place

annotations.note: A philosophical physical claim, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fire returning to its natural place

anchor.snippet: if there is a fire on the ground, the flames go up into the air

annotations.note: A physical observation, not a mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Aristotle's laws of motion

anchor.snippet: His laws of motion included: that heavier objects will fall faster

annotations.note: Physical laws of motion are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ibn al-Haytham's light rays

anchor.snippet: Ibn al-Haytham presented the idea of light rays as an alternative

annotations.note: An optics concept with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Kepler's laws of planetary motion

anchor.snippet: the laws governing the motion of planetary bodies

annotations.note: Grep found no 'Kepler' content in Mathlib; planetary motion laws are unformalized physics.

annotations.label: Newton's laws of motion and gravitation

anchor.snippet: Isaac Newton 's discovery and unification of the laws of motion and universal gravitation

annotations.note: Mathlib's 'Newton' files concern Newton's method/identities/polytope, not physical laws of motion or gravitation.

annotations.label: Calculus

anchor.snippet: the mathematical study of continuous change

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes derivatives/integrals but not 'calculus' as a named field-definition statement.

annotations.label: Classical physics

anchor.snippet: Taken together these theories became the basis for what would later be called classical physics

annotations.note: Classical physics as a discipline is not a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Luminiferous aether

anchor.snippet: Classical electromagnetism presumed a medium, an luminiferous aether to support the propagation of waves

annotations.note: A discredited physical hypothesis with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Special relativity

anchor.snippet: which replaced classical mechanics for fast-moving bodies

annotations.note: Mathlib's Lorentz/Minkowski files are geometry-of-numbers/gauge results, not relativistic physics.

annotations.label: Planck quantization

anchor.snippet: Planck proposed that the excitation of material oscillators is possible only in discrete steps

annotations.note: A quantum physics hypothesis not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Central theories of physics

anchor.snippet: These include classical mechanics, quantum mechanics

annotations.note: A list of physical theories, not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Domain of classical physics

anchor.section: Distinction between classical and modern physics
anchor.snippet: The laws of classical physics accurately describe systems whose important length scales are greater than the atomic scale

annotations.note: A statement about physical regimes, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Classical physics

anchor.snippet: Classical physics includes the traditional branches and topics that were recognized and well-developed before the beginning of the 20th century

annotations.note: A disciplinary definition with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Classical mechanics

anchor.snippet: Classical mechanics is concerned with bodies acted on by forces and bodies in motion

annotations.note: Classical mechanics is a physics field, not a Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Acoustics

anchor.snippet: Acoustics is the study of how sound is produced

annotations.note: Acoustics as a study area has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Ultrasonics

anchor.snippet: the study of sound waves of very high frequency

annotations.note: A subfield of acoustics, not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optics

anchor.snippet: Optics, the study of light

annotations.note: Optics is a physics field with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Heat and thermodynamics

anchor.snippet: thermodynamics deals with the relationships between heat and other forms of energy

annotations.note: Thermodynamics is a physics field, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Electromagnetic induction relation

anchor.snippet: an electric current gives rise to a magnetic field

annotations.note: Electromagnetic laws are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Electrostatics, electrodynamics, magnetostatics

anchor.snippet: Electrostatics deals with electric charges at rest

annotations.note: These electromagnetic subfields have no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Modern physics

anchor.snippet: with the latter category including effects related to quantum mechanics and relativity

annotations.note: Modern physics as a category is not a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Continuous vs. discrete nature

anchor.snippet: Classical mechanics approximates nature as continuous

annotations.note: A descriptive physical claim, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Special and general relativity

anchor.snippet: the special theory of relativity is concerned with motion in the absence of gravitational fields

annotations.note: Relativity theory is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scientific method

anchor.snippet: Physicists use the scientific method to test the validity of a physical theory

annotations.note: An epistemological process, not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scientific law

anchor.snippet: A scientific law is a concise verbal or mathematical statement of a relation that expresses a fundamental principle of some theory

annotations.note: A meta-definition of scientific laws, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Phenomenologists

anchor.snippet: Physicists who work at the interplay of theory and experiment are called phenomenologists

annotations.note: A profession descriptor with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Physics as the fundamental science

anchor.snippet: Therefore, physics is sometimes called the

annotations.note: A characterization of physics, not a mathematical object.

annotations.label: Lodestone magnetism

anchor.snippet: certain rocks ( lodestone and magnetite ) were attracted to one another by an invisible force

annotations.note: A physical observation about magnetism, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Amber and electricity

anchor.snippet: when rubbed with fur would cause a similar invisible attraction

annotations.note: A physical observation about static electricity, with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Electroweak unification

anchor.snippet: electromagnetism and the weak nuclear force are now considered to be two aspects of the electroweak interaction

annotations.note: A physical unification theory not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Particle physics

anchor.snippet: Particle physics is the study of the elementary constituents of matter and energy

annotations.note: Particle physics is a science field, not a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Standard Model description

anchor.snippet: the interactions of elementary particles and fields are described by the Standard Model

annotations.note: Grep found no Standard Model content; this physics theory is unformalized.

annotations.label: Twelve known particles of matter

anchor.snippet: The model accounts for the 12 known particles of matter

annotations.note: An empirical physics fact, not a mathematical statement in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nuclear physics

anchor.snippet: Nuclear physics is the field of physics that studies the constituents and interactions of atomic nuclei

annotations.note: A physics subfield with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Atomic, molecular, and optical physics

anchor.snippet: Atomic, molecular , and optical physics (AMO) is the study of matter

annotations.note: A physics subfield, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Atomic physics

anchor.snippet: Atomic physics studies the electron shells of atoms

annotations.note: A physics subfield with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Molecular physics

anchor.snippet: Molecular physics focuses on multi-atomic structures

annotations.note: A physics subfield not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Optical physics

anchor.snippet: Optical physics is distinct from optics

annotations.note: A physics subfield with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Condensed matter physics

anchor.snippet: Condensed matter physics is the field of physics that deals with the macroscopic physical properties of matter

annotations.note: A physics subfield, not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Condensed phases

anchor.snippet: The most familiar examples of condensed phases are solids and liquids

annotations.note: A physical example about states of matter, not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Astrophysics and astronomy

anchor.snippet: Astrophysics and astronomy are the application of the theories and methods of physics

annotations.note: Astrophysics is a science field with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Physical cosmology

anchor.snippet: Physical cosmology is the study of the formation and evolution of the universe on its largest scales

annotations.note: Cosmology is a physics field, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Two pillars of the Big Bang model

anchor.snippet: The Big Bang model rests on two theoretical pillars

annotations.note: A cosmological model claim with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Physics education

anchor.snippet: Physics education or physics teaching refers to the education methods currently used to teach physics

annotations.note: An education topic, not a mathematical object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Physicist

anchor.snippet: A physicist is a scientist who specializes in the field of physics

annotations.note: A profession definition with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Synthetic vs. analytic statements

anchor.snippet: Thus physics statements are synthetic, while mathematical statements are analytic

annotations.note: A philosophy-of-science claim, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Mathematical physics

anchor.snippet: mathematical physics is the application of mathematics in physics

annotations.note: Mathematical physics as a discipline is not a Mathlib object.

annotations.label: Fundamental science

anchor.snippet: Physics is a branch of fundamental science

annotations.note: A classification of science, not formalizable in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Applied physics

anchor.snippet: Applied physics is a general term for physics research and development that is intended for a particular use

annotations.note: A disciplinary category with no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Statics in bridge building

anchor.snippet: statics, a subfield of mechanics , is used in the building of bridges

annotations.note: An engineering application of mechanics, not formalized in Mathlib.
